title: First Conditional — Real Possibilities
description: Express real future possibilities and their likely consequences using if + present simple + will.

items.example: If it rains tomorrow, I'll take an umbrella.

items.back: I'll take an umbrella if it rains.
items.example: She'll be late if she doesn't leave now. (no comma needed)

items.back: Unless you hurry, you'll miss the bus.
items.example: We won't succeed unless we work together.

items.front: when / as soon as (certainty instead of if)
items.back: When I get home, I'll call you.
items.example: As soon as the results come out, I'll let you know.

items.front: Other modal results: might / can / should
items.back: If it rains, we might cancel. / If you're free, you can join us.
items.example: If you study hard, you should pass the exam.

items.back: If she doesn't hurry, she'll be late.
items.example: If he doesn't apologise, I won't forgive him.

items.front: DO NOT use 'will' in the if-clause
items.back: If it WILL rain → WRONG. If it rains → CORRECT.
items.example: If the weather is good, we'll have a picnic. (NOT: if the weather will be good)

items.front: Warnings and advice with first conditional
items.back: If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.
items.example: If you don't save your work, you'll lose everything.

items.prompt: If she ___ for the job, she'll probably get it.
items.explanation: In the if-clause, use present simple (not will): 'If she applies…'

items.prompt: ___ you don't study, you ___ fail the exam.
items.explanation: 'If you don't study, you will fail.' First conditional with negative condition.

items.prompt: We ___ the match unless the star player recovers in time.
items.explanation: 'Unless' = if not. 'We won't win unless he recovers' = if he doesn't recover, we'll lose.

items.prompt: As soon as I ___ home, I'll start cooking dinner.
items.explanation: After 'as soon as', use present simple (like the if-clause): 'as soon as I get home'.

items.prompt: If you eat all that cake, you ___ feel sick later.
items.explanation: 'Might' is used when the result is possible but not 100% certain: you might feel sick.

items.sentence: If it ___ tomorrow, we'll cancel the outdoor event. (rain)
She ___ be late unless she leaves right now.
If you ___ hard enough, you'll definitely pass. (try)
As soon as the meeting ___, I'll call you straight away. (end)
If you ___ save your work regularly, you'll lose everything. (not)
